🔬 Science

Organisms and Environment

Discover how living things interact with each other and their surroundings!

📚 Unit 3 ⏱️ 8 min read

Unit 3: Organisms and Environment

Organisms are living things. Environment is everything around them. This unit explores how living things interact with their surroundings.

What is an Organism?

An organism is any living thing.

Type of OrganismExamples
PlantsMango tree, grass, rose
AnimalsDog, bird, fish
HumansPeople
MicroorganismsBacteria, fungi (visible under microscope)

Characteristics of Living Things

How do we know if something is alive? Living things have these seven characteristics:

CharacteristicWhat it meansExample
1. MovementCan change positionA dog runs, a plant turns towards the sun
2. RespirationUse oxygen to release energyBreathe in air
3. SensitivityRespond to changes around themTouch a leaf and it moves
4. GrowthBecome bigger or developA baby becomes an adult
5. ReproductionProduce young onesA cat gives birth to kittens
6. ExcretionRemove waste productsSweat, urine, dropping leaves
7. NutritionTake in food for energyEating plants or other animals

💡 Remember the word: MRS GREN – Movement, Respiration, Sensitivity, Growth, Reproduction, Excretion, Nutrition

Living vs. Non-living Things

Living Things ✅Non-living Things ❌
GrowDo not grow
ReproduceCannot reproduce
Need food and waterDo not need food or water
BreatheDo not breathe
Respond to changesDo not respond
Have a lifespan (born, live, die)Do not have a lifespan

Components of the Environment

The environment has two parts:

ComponentWhat it includesExamples
Biotic (Living)All living thingsPlants, animals, humans, bacteria
Abiotic (Non-living)All non-living thingsSunlight, water, air, soil, temperature

Biotic + Abiotic = Environment

Abiotic Factors

FactorImportance
SunlightPlants need it to make food (photosynthesis)
WaterAll living things need water to survive
AirProvides oxygen for breathing, carbon dioxide for plants
SoilPlants grow in soil; provides nutrients
TemperatureAffects where organisms can live

Habitat and Adaptation

Habitat

A habitat is the natural home of an organism.

HabitatDescriptionOrganisms found there
ForestLand with many treesTiger, deer, monkey, birds
DesertVery dry, sandyCamel, cactus, lizard
OceanSalt waterFish, whale, dolphin, coral
PondFresh waterFrog, duck, lotus, fish
GrasslandOpen grassy areasLion, zebra, elephant
Polar regionVery coldPolar bear, penguin, seal

Adaptation

Adaptation is the special feature that helps an organism survive in its habitat.

HabitatAnimalAdaptationHow it helps
DesertCamelHump stores fat, long eyelashesFood storage, sand protection
PolarPolar bearThick fur, layer of fatKeeps warm
OceanFishGills, finsBreathe underwater, swim
ForestMonkeyLong arms, strong tailClimb trees
PondFrogWebbed feet, long tongueSwim, catch insects
DesertCactusThick stem, spinesStore water, reduce water loss

💡 Did you know? A cactus can store water for months!

Food Chain

A food chain shows who eats whom in nature.

Food Chain Components

LevelNameWhat they doExample
1ProducerMake their own food (plants)Grass, tree, algae
2Primary ConsumerEat plants (herbivores)Rabbit, cow, deer
3Secondary ConsumerEat herbivores (carnivores)Snake, fox
4Tertiary ConsumerTop predatorsLion, eagle
5DecomposerBreak down dead thingsFungi, bacteria

Example Food Chains

Forest Food Chain:

Grass → Rabbit → Snake → Eagle (Producer) (Herbivore) (Carnivore) (Top predator)

Pond Food Chain:

Algae → Small fish → Big fish → Heron

💡 Remember: Energy flows from the sun → plants → animals.

Ecosystem

An ecosystem is a community of living things interacting with their non-living environment.

Types of Ecosystems

TypeExamples
Natural ecosystemForest, ocean, desert, grassland, pond
Artificial ecosystemGarden, aquarium, farm, zoo

Balance in Nature

Balance in nature means all living and non-living things are in harmony.

What keeps balanceExplanation
Food chainsAnimals eat plants and each other
DecomposersBreak down dead things into nutrients
Oxygen and carbon dioxide cyclePlants give oxygen, animals give carbon dioxide

What Happens When Balance is Disturbed?

DisturbanceEffect
Cutting too many treesAnimals lose homes, less oxygen
Killing too many predatorsToo many herbivores, plants get eaten
PollutionHarms plants and animals
Too many of one animalFood becomes scarce

Environmental Problems and Solutions

Major Environmental Problems

ProblemCauseEffect
DeforestationCutting treesSoil erosion, loss of habitat
PollutionWaste, smoke, chemicalsHarm to living things
Global warmingToo much carbon dioxideEarth becomes hotter
Water scarcityWasting water, droughtNo water for drinking or farming

How Can We Help?

ActionWhy it helps
Plant treesMore oxygen, homes for animals
Reduce, Reuse, Recycle (3 Rs)Less waste
Save waterMore water for everyone
Keep surroundings cleanLess pollution
Do not waste electricitySaves energy

🌍 Remember: Earth is our only home. Take care of it!

Try It Yourself!

Exercise 1: Fill in the Blanks

  1. A living thing is called an ____________.
  2. The natural home of an organism is its ____________.
  3. A ____________ shows who eats whom in nature.
  4. Green plants are called ____________ because they make their own food.
  5. ____________ are living things. ____________ are non-living things.

Exercise 2: Living or Non-living?

Write “Living” or “Non-living” for each.

  1. A dog ______
  2. A table ______
  3. A rose plant ______
  4. Sunlight ______
  5. A fish ______

Exercise 3: Complete the Food Chain

Complete each food chain by filling in the blank.

  1. Grass → _________ → Snake
  2. Algae → Small fish → _________
  3. _________ → Cow → Lion

Exercise 4: Match the Animal to Its Adaptation

AnimalAdaptation
Camela) Thick fur
Polar bearb) Hump
Fishc) Gills
Cactusd) Long tongue to catch insects
Froge) Spines to store water

Exercise 5: Answer in One Sentence

  1. What is an environment?
  2. Why do plants need sunlight?
  3. What happens if we cut too many trees?

Answer Key

Exercise 1: 1. organism, 2. habitat, 3. food chain, 4. producers, 5. Biotic, Abiotic

Exercise 2: 1. Living, 2. Non-living, 3. Living, 4. Non-living, 5. Living

Exercise 3: 1. Rabbit, 2. Big fish, 3. Grass

Exercise 4: Camel→b, Polar bear→a, Fish→c, Cactus→e, Frog→d

Great work! You now understand how living things interact with their environment. Be a friend to nature!
